essence: SaaS sells software as a subscription: build once, sell many times - near-zero marginal cost makes gross margins very high (~70-85%).
The model lives or dies on retention: recurring revenue only compounds if customers stay (net revenue retention >100% = growth even without new logos).
It's a CAC-vs-LTV game: spend upfront to acquire, earn it back over years - so payback period and churn decide the economics.
'Land and expand': get in cheap, then grow seats/usage/upsell - expansion is the cheapest growth.
AI is both tailwind (new products) and threat (commoditising features, pricing pressure).

definition.oneLiner: Companies that deliver software over the cloud on a subscription rather than selling licences - continuously updated and centrally hosted.
definition.jobToBeDone: Help businesses (or people) do a job better - automate a workflow, manage data, collaborate - via always-on software they rent instead of own.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit in retained, expanding subscriptions at scale - once R&D and go-to-market are spent, incremental revenue is near-pure margin; the deepest pools are in sticky, mission-critical enterprise software with high switching costs.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from seat-based licences -> usage/consumption pricing -> AI-embedded and outcome-based pricing, as AI changes both the product and how it's charged.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Annual recurring revenue per customer (ARR / ACV)
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Customers x ACV. ARR grows via new logos, seat/usage expansion, price, and - crucially - retention; net revenue retention compounds the base.

costStructure.shape: High fixed R&D and go-to-market spend, very low marginal cost to serve an extra customer (just cloud) - so gross margins are high (~70-85%) but profitability depends on growth efficiency.
costStructure.leverage: Enormous operating leverage as ARR scales over a fixed cost base - but only if churn is low and CAC is recovered; otherwise S&M spend outruns retained revenue.

moats: High switching costs (data, workflows, integrations embedded)
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Category-by-category: each function tends toward a few category leaders, but the overall market is vast and fragmented with constant new entrants.

structuralForces.regulation: Lighter than finance/telecom but rising - data privacy (GDPR, India DPDP), data residency, security/compliance certifications (SOC2, ISO), and emerging AI regulation.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Sensitive to enterprise IT budgets and the funding cycle: downturns slow new bookings and raise churn; rate cycles shift the growth-vs-profit balance.
structuralForces.substitution: Build-vs-buy (in-house software), open-source alternatives, incumbents bundling features for free, and increasingly AI tools that replicate point-solution functionality.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Cloud providers (AWS/Azure/GCP) are powerful suppliers (hosting cost); large enterprise buyers negotiate hard; developers/champions drive bottom-up adoption.

aiPenetration.maturity: High and rising fast - AI is reshaping both how software is built (copilots) and what it does (AI features now table-stakes).
keyUseCases: AI copilots & assistants embedded in the product
Agentic workflows that act, not just inform
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI both raises product value (new capabilities, premium pricing) and pressures it (commoditising features, shifting to usage/outcome pricing); it also slashes engineering and support costs.
threatsDisruption: Customers building their own with AI

benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Reduce churn / raise net revenue retention
Improve CAC payback & sales efficiency (right GTM motion)
Improve gross margin (cloud cost, support automation)

keyTerms.ARR / MRR: Annual / Monthly Recurring Revenue - the subscription run-rate.
keyTerms.ACV: Annual Contract Value - average annualised revenue per customer.
keyTerms.Churn: Rate at which customers (logo churn) or revenue cancel.
keyTerms.NRR: Net Revenue Retention - revenue from existing customers incl. expansion minus churn; >100% means the base grows by itself.
keyTerms.CAC / LTV: Customer Acquisition Cost vs Lifetime Value.
keyTerms.CAC payback: Months of gross profit needed to recover acquisition cost.
keyTerms.Rule of 40: Growth% plus profit margin% should be >= 40 for a healthy SaaS.
keyTerms.PLG: Product-Led Growth - the product itself drives acquisition (self-serve).
keyTerms.Seat / usage pricing: Charging per user (seat) vs per consumption (usage).

questionsToAskFirst: What's net revenue retention - is the base growing or leaking?
CAC payback and LTV:CAC - is growth efficient?
What's the GTM motion and does it fit the ACV?
Where on the Rule of 40 does it sit?
How exposed is the product to AI commoditisation?
rookieTraps: Chasing new-logo growth while ignoring churn (leaky bucket)
Funding growth with S&M that never pays back
Ignoring NRR - the real engine of SaaS compounding

whatsUniqueHere: SaaS is the closest thing business has to a compounding machine: build the software once, sell it forever, and let retained revenue stack on top of itself. That magic only works if customers stay and spend more over time - so the whole strategy reduces to retention and efficient acquisition. The trap is that high gross margins hide a leaky bucket: growth bought with sales dollars that churns away is an illusion of progress.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: India is a global SaaS force on two fronts: a thriving product-SaaS ecosystem selling to the world (Zoho, Freshworks, Postman) and the giant IT-services industry (TCS, Infosys) pivoting toward platforms and AI; deep, cost-effective engineering talent underpins both.
majorPlayers: TCS / Infosys (IT services pivoting to SaaS/AI)
policyTailwinds: Global SaaS demand + India cost arbitrage
demandDrivers: Global B2B SaaS demand served from India
structuralChallenges: Selling to global markets from India (GTM distance)
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Fintech & Payments - tech-driven, CAC/LTV, network effects

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: Global SaaS is being reshaped by AI: incumbents racing to embed copilots, usage/outcome pricing rising, and AI-native startups challenging point solutions. India's leaders (Zoho, Freshworks) and the IT-services giants are repositioning around AI; funding favours efficient growth (Rule of 40) over growth-at-all-costs.
